params.text:
What a data breach looks like

A personal data breach is any event where personal information is lost, stolen, seen by the wrong person, or destroyed by accident. It is not always dramatic. A care record emailed to the wrong address, a paper file left on a bus, or a private conversation overheard in a corridor can all be breaches.

Serious breaches must be reported to the ICO, sometimes within a tight time limit, so acting quickly really matters. You are not expected to make that legal judgement on your own — but you are expected to raise it without delay.

params.alt: Illustration for Breaches and a scenario

params.text:
Putting it together

You finish a visit to Mrs Okafor and realise the daily notes you printed for handover are missing from your bag. You think you may have left them at the previous person’s home. What should you do?

  • Act quickly: tell your manager straight away rather than waiting to see if the notes turn up.
  • Be honest: explain exactly what happened, without playing it down.
  • Record the facts: note what was lost, when, and what you have done since.
  • Learn from it: agree how to carry records more safely in future.

Reporting your own mistake is not a failure — it is what protects Mrs Okafor and helps your organisation put things right.

params.question: You realise you may have left a person’s printed care notes at another address. What should you do first?

answers.text: Report it to your manager straight away and be honest about what happened.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Reporting promptly and honestly lets your organisation act within any legal time limits and reduce the harm.

answers.text: Wait a day or two to see whether the notes are handed back before saying anything.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Waiting risks missing a reporting deadline and leaves the person’s data exposed for longer.

answers.text: Say nothing, as admitting the mistake could get you into trouble.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Hiding a breach is far more serious than the loss itself and breaks your duty to be open.

answers.text: Deal with it yourself by retracing your route, without telling anyone.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. You may retrace your steps, but the breach must still be reported so it can be handled properly.
